title: Slab jewellery dish in air-dry clay
subtitle: A small square dish with folded-up edges for jewellery storage
excerpt: A slab jewellery dish is made from a single rolled slab with the corners cut, the edges folded upward, and the corners pinched together. It takes under 30 minutes to form and dries flat overnight. The result is a small, shallow tray that holds rings, studs, and small pendants. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Slab origami-fold form. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: jewellery dish is assembled from a single piece of clay without any joins other than the four corner seams. Cutting a square or rectangle, trimming the corners diagonally, folding up the sides, and pinching the corners closed produces a neat tray form in one step. The technique is fast and the result is consistent.

attrs.body: Press stamps, leaf impressions, or sgraffito lines into the slab surface while it is flat on the board. It is much easier to work on a flat surface than on a formed dish. Avoid decorating within 2 cm of the edges, which will become the sides and corners.
attrs.title: Decorate before folding

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 6 mm guide rails

content.text: Step 1: Roll and cut the slab

Roll the clay to 6 mm thick. Cut a 16 x 16 cm square with the fettling knife and ruler. At each corner, cut a square notch 3 x 3 cm out of the corner: this is the material that becomes the corner join. The four cuts leave a cross-shaped slab.
While the slab is still flat, press a stamp, rolled lace, or a leaf into the centre section. The centre 10 x 10 cm becomes the base of the finished dish. Keep all impressions within this area.

Fold one side of the slab upward to 90 degrees. Hold it in position. Fold the adjacent side up in the same way. The two cut edges of the corner notch now sit next to each other, forming a vertical corner seam.
both faces of the corner seam and press together. Pinch and smooth the joint on both the interior and exterior. Repeat for all four corners.

Smooth the exterior of the corners with a damp finger and check that all four sides are at 90 degrees by sitting the dish on a flat board. Adjust any leaning walls by pressing gently before the clay firms. Allow to dry bone-dry over 24 to 48 hours. Seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1.
Cut a rectangle instead of a square for an elongated tray, 16 x 22 cm makes a dish suitable for a watch and keys.
Fold the sides at 60 degrees rather than 90 for a more open, shallower tray profile.

attrs.intro: Problems when making the slab jewellery dish.

items.fix: Score both crack faces, apply fresh slurry, press and pinch firmly. Dry slowly under loose plastic wrap for the first 12 hours.
items.cause: The seam was not scored and slipped, or pinched too lightly.
items.symptom: Corner seams crack open as the dish dries

items.fix: Let the slab sit uncovered for 20 to 30 minutes before folding, it should be soft but no longer floppy. Prop the sides against books while the corner seams firm.
items.cause: Sides were folded before the slab had firmed slightly.
items.symptom: Sides lean outward or are not straight

attrs.heading: Common problems
